#pragma once

#include <rfl.hpp>

namespace schema_manage_indexes {

    std::string sku;
    std::string name;
    std::string category;
    double price;
  };

  bool manage_indexes() {
    // Connect to the SQLite database
    auto conn_result = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    if (!conn_result) {
      return false;
    }
    auto conn = *conn_result;

    // Create the Product table
    auto create_table_result = sqlgen::create_table<Product>(conn);
    if (!create_table_result) {
      return false;
    }

    // Create the indexes with IF NOT EXISTS logic
    auto idx_category = sqlgen::create_index<"idx_product_category", Product>("category") | sqlgen::if_not_exists;
    auto idx_sku = sqlgen::create_index<"idx_product_sku", Product>("sku") | sqlgen::if_not_exists | sqlgen::unique;
    auto idx_name_price = sqlgen::create_index<"idx_product_name_price", Product>("name", "price") | sqlgen::if_not_exists;

    // Execute the index creation statements
    auto idx_category_result = idx_category(conn);
    if (!idx_category_result) {
      return false;
    }

    auto idx_sku_result = idx_sku(conn);
    if (!idx_sku_result) {
      return false;
    }

    auto idx_name_price_result = idx_name_price(conn);
    if (!idx_name_price_result) {
      return false;
    }

    return true;
  }

}  // namespace schema_manage_indexes
```</answer>

  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;

The reference materials for the update function show:

template <class TableType, class SetsType, class WhereType = Nothing>
struct Update {
  auto operator()(const auto& _conn) const {
    return update_impl<TableType, SetsType, WhereType>(_conn, sets_, where_);
  }

  template <class ConditionType>
  friend auto operator|(const Update& _u, const Where<ConditionType>& _where) {
    static_assert(std::is_same_v<WhereType, Nothing>,
                  "You cannot call where(...) twice (but you can apply more "
                  "than one condition by combining them with && or ||).");
    return Update<TableType, SetsType, ConditionType>{
        .sets_ = _u.sets_, .where_ = _where.condition};
  }

  SetsType sets_;

  WhereType where_;
};

And the update function is:

template <class TableType, class... SetsType>
auto update(const SetsType&... _sets) {
  static_assert(sizeof...(_sets) > 0, "You must update at least one column.");
  using TupleType = rfl::Tuple<std::remove_cvref_t<SetsType>...>;
  return Update<TableType, TupleType>{.sets_ = TupleType(_sets...)};
};

}

#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>

namespace schema_create_view_sales_summary {

  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
  int stock;
};

struct Sale {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int, sqlgen::auto_incr> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  double amount;
};

struct SalesSummary {
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  std::string product_name;
  int total_quantity;
  double total_revenue;
};

/**
 * Function: Create a view SalesSummary that aggregates sales by product.
 * 
 * @return true if view creation succeeds, false otherwise
 */
bool create_view_sales_summary() {
  // Connect to the SQLite database
  auto conn_result = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  if (!conn_result) {
    return false;
  }
  auto conn = conn_result.value();

  // Build the SELECT query for the view
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;
  auto select_query = sqlgen::select_from<Sale>("s")
      .join<Product>("p", "s.product_id"_c == "p.product_id"_c)

  // Execute the statement and return the result

}

}  // namespace schema_create_view_sales_summary

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace replace_text_in_notes {

struct Note {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> note_id;
  std::string content;
};

struct UpdatedNote {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> note_id;
  std::string updated_content;
};

/**
 * description: replace_text_in_notes.
 * This function demonstrates how to replace text in notes using sqlgen update API.
 * It replaces all occurrences of "foo" with "bar" in the content column of the Note table.
 * Returns: std::vector<UpdatedNote>.
 */
std::vector<UpdatedNote> replace_text_in_notes() {
  // Connect to the SQLite database
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  // Create update query to replace "foo" with "bar" in content column
  auto update_query = sqlgen::update<Note> | sqlgen::set(
      Note::content = sqlgen::replace(Note::content, "foo", "bar")
  );
  
  // Execute the update
  update_query(conn);
  
  // Create select query to retrieve updated notes with content aliased as updated_content
  auto select_query = sqlgen::select_from<Note>(Note::note_id, Note::content.as("updated_content"));
  
  // Execute the select and return results
  return select_query(conn).read<std::vector<UpdatedNote>>;
}

}  // namespace replace_text_in_notes
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace inventory_bulk_write_and_read {

struct Inventory {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  std::string name;
  int quantity;
  std::string location;
};

/**
 * description: inventory_bulk_write_and_read.
 * This function demonstrates how to perform bulk write and read operations on an inventory table.
 * It first drops and recreates the table, then writes the given items to the table.
 * Finally, it reads back the items with quantity > 50, orders them by quantity in descending order,
 * and returns true if at least one item is found.
 * Returns: bool.
 */
bool inventory_bulk_write_and_read(const std::vector<Inventory>& items) {
  // Connect to the SQLite database
  auto conn_result = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  if (!conn_result) {
    return false;
  }
  auto conn = *conn_result;

  // Bulk write the items to the database
  auto write_result = sqlgen::write(conn, items);
  if (!write_result) {
    return false;
  }

  // Build the query to read items with quantity > 50, ordered by quantity descending
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;
  auto query = sqlgen::read<std::vector<Inventory>>
               | sqlgen::where("quantity"_c > 50)
               | sqlgen::order_by("quantity"_c.desc());

  // Execute the query and check if any items were found
  auto read_result = query(conn);
  if (!read_result) {
    return false;
  }

  return read_result->size() >= 1;
}

}  // namespace inventory_bulk_write_and_read

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <unordered_map>

namespace update_inventory_stock {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  int stock;
};

bool update_inventory_stock(const std::unordered_map<int, int>& received) {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;
  auto conn_res = sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  if (!conn_res) return false;

  auto txn_res = begin_transaction(conn_res.value());
  if (!txn_res) return false;
  auto txn = txn_res.value();
  
  for (const auto& [pid, qty] : received) {
    std::string sql = "UPDATE Product SET stock = stock + " + std::to_string(qty) +
                      " WHERE product_id = " + std::to_string(pid) + ";";
    auto exec_res = exec(txn, sql);
    if (!exec_res) {
      return false;
    }
  }
  
  auto commit_res = commit(txn);
  return commit_res.has_value();
}

}  // namespace update_inventory_stock
```

We are given the task to write a function named `drop_table_if_exists` that uses the sqlgen library to construct a DROP TABLE statement with the `IF EXISTS` check.

Steps:
1. Define a `Log` struct. This is already provided in the code snippet.
2. Construct a query to drop the `Log` table if it exists.
3. Return the generated SQL string.

We note that the `sqlgen` library provides a way to generate SQL statements by using its fluent interface. We can use the `drop` function from the `sqlgen` namespace and then chain the `if_exists` modifier using the `operator|` to set the `if_exists_` flag to true.

The `drop` function returns a `Drop` object, which we can then modify with `if_exists` and then convert to SQL using the `to_sql` function from the `sqlgen::sqlite` namespace.

Let's break down the steps in the function:

- We need to create a `Drop` object for the `Log` table. We can do this by calling `sqlgen::drop<Log>()`.
- Then, we want to set the `if_exists` flag, so we use the `operator|` to combine the `Drop` object with `sqlgen::if_exists` (which is of type `IfExists`). This will set the `if_exists_` member of the `Drop` object to true.
- Finally, we convert the resulting object to SQL by passing it to `sqlgen::sqlite::to_sql`.

The function should return the generated SQL string.

Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace drop_table_if_exists {

struct Log {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  std::string message;
};

/**
 * This function demonstrates dropping a table if it exists.
 * Returns: std::string.
 */
inline std::string drop_table_if_exists() {
    auto drop_stmt = sqlgen::drop<Log> | sqlgen::if_exists;
    return sqlgen::sqlite::to_sql(drop_stmt);
}

}  // namespace drop_table_if_exists
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace project_time_tracking {

struct Project {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> project_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct TimeLog {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> log_id;
  int project_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> start_time;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> end_time;
};

struct TimeMetrics {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> project_id;
  std::string name;
  int day;
  int month;
  int year;
  int hour;
  int minute;
  int second;
  double duration_days;
};

std::vector<TimeMetrics> project_time_tracking() {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;
  
  auto query = sqlgen::select_from<Project, TimeLog>(
        Project.project_id, Project.name,
        TimeLog.start_time.extract_day().as("day"),
        TimeLog.start_time.extract_month().as("month"),
        TimeLog.start_time.extract_year().as("year"),
        TimeLog.start_time.extract_hour().as("hour"),
        TimeLog.start_time.extract_minute().as("minute"),
        TimeLog.start_time.extract_second().as("second"),
        sqlgen::days_between(TimeLog.start_time, TimeLog.end_time).as("duration_days")
    )
    .join(TimeLog, Project.project_id == TimeLog.project_id)
    .group_by(Project.project_id, TimeLog.start_time.week());
  
  auto result = query(conn);
  
  std::vector<TimeMetrics> metrics;
  for (auto& row : result) {
    metrics.emplace_back(
        TimeMetrics{
            .project_id = row.project_id,
            .name = row.name,
            .day = row.day,
            .month = row.month,
            .year = row.year,
            .hour = row.hour,
            .minute = row.minute,
            .second = row.second,
            .duration_days = row.duration_days
        });
  }
  return metrics;
}

}  // namespace project_time_tracking
```</answer>
